questions.stem: This high-power field shows a single shrunken, intensely pink (eosinophilic) cell with a condensed nucleus surrounded by intact squamous cells. Which feature confirms this is apoptosis rather than necrosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lack of a surrounding inflammatory infiltrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diffuse cellular swelling of the neighboring cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of tissue architecture across the field

explanation.summary: Apoptotic cells shrink, become densely eosinophilic, and show condensed nuclei; because apoptosis is controlled and cells are cleared by phagocytes, there is little or no inflammation. Necrosis, by contrast, spills cell contents and provokes a prominent inflammatory response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Apoptotic cells shrink, become densely eosinophilic, and show condensed nuclei; because apoptosis is controlled and cells are cleared by phagocytes, there is little or no inflammation. Necrosis, by contrast, spills cell contents and provokes a prominent inflammatory response.

questions.stem: This schematic shows a viable cell kept alive by the anti-apoptotic protein BCL2 at the mitochondrion. Pathologic overexpression of this protein, via a t(14;18) translocation, characteristically drives which malignancy?

explanation.summary: BCL2 keeps the outer mitochondrial membrane impermeable, blocking cytochrome c release and preventing apoptosis. The t(14;18) translocation places BCL2 under an immunoglobulin promoter, causing overexpression that resists apoptosis and produces follicular lymphoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: BCL2 keeps the outer mitochondrial membrane impermeable, blocking cytochrome c release and preventing apoptosis. The t(14;18) translocation places BCL2 under an immunoglobulin promoter, causing overexpression that resists apoptosis and produces follicular lymphoma.

questions.stem: The left gross panel shows a pale yellow wedge-shaped lesion and the right microscopic panel shows preserved tissue outlines with loss of nuclei. This pattern of necrosis is most typical of which underlying cause?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ischemia (loss of blood supply) outside the CNS

explanation.summary: Coagulative necrosis preserves the tissue architecture (ghost outlines) while cells die, and is the hallmark of ischemic infarction in solid organs other than the brain. The wedge-shaped pale infarct and retained cell outlines are classic.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coagulative necrosis preserves the tissue architecture (ghost outlines) while cells die, and is the hallmark of ischemic infarction in solid organs other than the brain. The wedge-shaped pale infarct and retained cell outlines are classic.

questions.stem: This gross brain specimen shows necrotic tissue that has been converted into a soft, semi-liquid, cavitated mass. What mechanism accounts for this liquefied consistency?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enzymatic (hydrolytic) digestion of the dead tissue

explanation.summary: Liquefactive necrosis results when hydrolytic enzymes digest dead tissue into a viscous liquid. It is the characteristic pattern of central nervous system infarcts and of abscesses. Protein denaturation instead yields coagulative necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Liquefactive necrosis results when hydrolytic enzymes digest dead tissue into a viscous liquid. It is the characteristic pattern of central nervous system infarcts and of abscesses. Protein denaturation instead yields coagulative necrosis.

questions.stem: This clinical photograph shows blackened, mummified digits. This descriptive term for necrosis is most commonly applied in the setting of what?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ischemia of a limb or its digits

explanation.summary: Gangrenous necrosis is a clinical term for necrosis of a limb or digits, typically from loss of blood supply. Dry gangrene is largely coagulative; wet gangrene adds superimposed liquefactive necrosis from bacterial infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gangrenous necrosis is a clinical term for necrosis of a limb or digits, typically from loss of blood supply. Dry gangrene is largely coagulative; wet gangrene adds superimposed liquefactive necrosis from bacterial infection.

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows a soft, friable, cheese-like area of necrosis. This pattern is classically associated with infection by which organism?

explanation.summary: Caseous necrosis has a friable, cheese-like gross appearance and is classically caused by tuberculosis. Microscopically it shows granulomas with fragmented, granular debris, distinguishing it from other necrosis patterns.
explanation.whyCorrect: Caseous necrosis has a friable, cheese-like gross appearance and is classically caused by tuberculosis. Microscopically it shows granulomas with fragmented, granular debris, distinguishing it from other necrosis patterns.

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows adipose tissue studded with chalky-white deposits. These deposits form through saponification involving calcium and what other substance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Free fatty acids released from necrotic fat

explanation.summary: In fat necrosis, released lipases (e.g., in acute pancreatitis) break down adipose tissue; the liberated free fatty acids combine with calcium in a saponification reaction to form the chalky-white deposits seen here.
explanation.whyCorrect: In fat necrosis, released lipases (e.g., in acute pancreatitis) break down adipose tissue; the liberated free fatty acids combine with calcium in a saponification reaction to form the chalky-white deposits seen here.

questions.stem: This histology field shows a blood vessel wall with a bright pink, smudgy deposit and a surrounding inflammatory infiltrate. This pattern of necrosis is most characteristically seen in which condition?

explanation.summary: Fibrinoid necrosis occurs when immune complexes and plasma proteins (fibrin) deposit in vessel walls, producing a bright pink, smudgy appearance with inflammation. It is characteristic of autoimmune small-vessel vasculitides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fibrinoid necrosis occurs when immune complexes and plasma proteins (fibrin) deposit in vessel walls, producing a bright pink, smudgy appearance with inflammation. It is characteristic of autoimmune small-vessel vasculitides.

questions.stem: This flowchart shows how a cell responds as injury increases from adaptation through to cell death by necrosis or apoptosis. What primarily determines whether the injured cell adapts and recovers versus dies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cell's location within the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: The severity and persistence of the injurious stimulus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the cell contains a nucleus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the cell is haploid or diploid

explanation.summary: The diagram shows a continuum: mild, short-lived injury permits adaptation or reversible injury, whereas severe, prolonged injury exceeds the cell's capacity to recover and drives it to death by necrosis or apoptosis. Thus the intensity and duration of the stimulus is the key determinant.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram shows a continuum: mild, short-lived injury permits adaptation or reversible injury, whereas severe, prolonged injury exceeds the cell's capacity to recover and drives it to death by necrosis or apoptosis. Thus the intensity and duration of the stimulus is the key determinant.

questions.stem: This diagram outlines the major biochemical mechanisms of cell injury. A fall in ATP (far left) most directly compromises which cellular process?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activity of the plasma-membrane Na+/K+ ATPase, causing cell swelling

explanation.summary: ATP is required to run the Na+/K+ ATPase; when ATP falls, the pump fails, sodium and water accumulate intracellularly, and the cell swells. The other mechanisms shown (calcium influx, ROS, membrane damage, DNA damage) act through separate routes.
explanation.whyCorrect: ATP is required to run the Na+/K+ ATPase; when ATP falls, the pump fails, sodium and water accumulate intracellularly, and the cell swells. The other mechanisms shown (calcium influx, ROS, membrane damage, DNA damage) act through separate routes.
